coredata.dc:title: Bismuth vanadate/graphene quantum dot: A new nanocomposite for photoelectrochemical determination of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Prado, Thiago M.
Carrico, Amanda
Cincotto, Fernando H.
Fatibello-Filho, Orlando
Moraes, Fernando C.
coredata.dc:description: A photoelectrochemical sensor for dopamine was fabricated by the immobilization of bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) and graphene quantum dots on a fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy experiments demonstrated that the presence of graphene quantum dots contributed to the decrease in charge transfer resistance of the sensor under irradiation of visible light in the presence of dopamine. The photocurrent signals obtained were proportional to the dopamine concentration from 3.6 × 10−8 to 2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1, with a detection limit estimated at 8.2 × 10−9 mol L−1 and a limit of quantification at 2.7 × 10−8 mol L−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bismuth vanadate
Reduced graphene oxide
Dopamine
Visible light
Photoelectrochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermal deposition of BiVO4 onto an FTO plate
Modification of the FTO/BiVO4 electrode with GQDs
Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical behaviour of the BiVO4/GQDs
Dopamine photoelectrochemical response over FTO/BiVO4/GQDs electrode
Analytical performance of the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation of reaction of DA on FTO/BiVO4/GQDs under visible light irradiation.
FEG image of BiVO4 thermal deposited film (A). Inset (A) TEM image of BiVO4 particles; and HRTEM image of BiVO4 modified with GQDs (B). Inset HRTEM image of GQDs over BiVO4 surface.

Linear sweep voltammetry for the FTO (blue line), FTO/BiVO4 (red line) and FTO/BiVO4/GQDs (black line) recorded at potential range from 0.0 V to +1.0 V with a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 using an on/off step of irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Linear sweep voltammetry of the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs without irradiation (red line) and under irradiation (red circle) and FTO/BiVO4 without irradiation (black line) and under irradiation (black square) (A). Scan rate 50 mV s−1; Nyquist plot of the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs (red circle) and FTO/BiVO4 (black square). Frequency range applied from 1 MHz to 10 mHz. All experiments were performed in presence of 0.4 μmol L−1 DA at borate buffer solution pH 9.0. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Photocurrent responses of FTO a); FTO/BiVO4 b) and FTO/BiVO4/GQDs c) from the amperometry (on/off) experiments at +0.4 V in presence of 0.4 mmol L−1 DA at borate buffer solution pH 9.0.

Amperograms of FTO/BiVO4/QGDs photoelectrochemical sensor recorded at potential +0.4 V and borate buffer solution pH 9.0 (A). Calibration curve obtained from ΔI (B). DA concentration range, from 3.6 × 10−8 to 2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1.
Analytical characteristics of the different electrochemical sensors for dopamine determination.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: LOD: Limit of detection; DPV: differential pulse voltammetry; POMA-Au: poly(o-methoxyaniline)-gold; SWCNT/PET: single-walled carbon nanotube transparent ﬂexible sensor; FSF: free-standing single-walled carbon nanotube film. AuAg@Au/RGO– C3N4: gold and silver nanoparticles; @Au gold core shell nanoparticles; RGO-C3N4: reduced graphene oxide and graphitic carbon nitride; Fe3O4@SiO2-NH-EDTA-Tb(III): Fe3O4 nanoparticle; SiO2-NH-EDTA: functionalized silicon dioxide and terbium; WO3: tungsten trioxide; CV: cyclic voltammetry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for DA standard recovery from urine spiked samples. Experiments performed in borate buffer solution at pH 9.0.

head.ce:title: Bismuth vanadate/graphene quantum dot: A new nanocomposite for photoelectrochemical determination of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of São Carlos, São Carlos, SP, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Federal University of Rio de Janeiro

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A photoelectrochemical sensor for dopamine was fabricated by the immobilization of bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) and graphene quantum dots on a fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy experiments demonstrated that the presence of graphene quantum dots contributed to the decrease in charge transfer resistance of the sensor under irradiation of visible light in the presence of dopamine. The photocurrent signals obtained were proportional to the dopamine concentration from 3.6 × 10−8 to 2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1, with a detection limit estimated at 8.2 × 10−9 mol L−1 and a limit of quantification at 2.7 × 10−8 mol L−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Catecholamines such as dopamine (DA), epinephrine (EP), norepinephrine (NEP), and serotonin (SE) are neurotransmitters with an important role in the central nervous system [1]. Pathological studies and diagnosis of certain diseases are based on these neurotransmitters. Measurements of urinary free catecholamine (UFCA) are widely regarded as sensitive and specific screening tests to detect brains tumours, such as pheochromocytoma and neuroblastoma [2]. The occurrence of these diseases in children is diagnosed by the presence of DA at high levels in urine, and in this case the other neurotransmitters are found at low levels. The calculated levels of EP and NEP over the total amount of free catecholamines are about 0.7% and 1.6%, respectively [3]. Therefore, it is considered that the detection of free dopamine is not significantly affected by other neurotransmitters (EP and NEP), which is an important characteristic that was considered for the development of this work.

Additionally, the levels of neurotransmitters in bodily fluids provide records useful for detecting heart and circulatory diseases, as well as diabetes mellitus [4,5]. Catecholamine concentrations in bodily fluids can serve as biochemical indicators of neurological disorders related to memory formation and learning, and they are useful in the investigation of the pathological process of Parkinson’s disease [6].

Usually, the analytical methods employed for the determination of dopamine in clinical analyses are based on chromatographic techniques using different detection systems [7,8]. These methods often require several previous sample preparation steps (e.g. extraction and clean up procedures) and the application for continuous in situ analysis is not easy. In these techniques, waste-containing organic solvents are generated. Electrochemical methods are promising for DA detection at low levels and have the following advantages: low cost, easy operation and high sensitivity, potential miniaturization and automation, the possibility for construction of simple portable devices for in-field monitoring, and fast screening purposes [9–11].

Electrochemically modified sensors based on nanostructured materials with electrocatalytic properties or with electron mediators were employed for the determination of dopamine in biological fluid samples (e.g. urine) [12–14]. Photo-assisted electrochemical sensors are alternative devices for dopamine quantification, and their responses are based on the interaction between light and nanostructured materials with fluorimetrical or semiconductor features [15–17]. The on/off photocurrent responses, present constant signal baseline with high reproducibility and low standard deviation, which improves the calculation of the limit of detection and limit of quantification. This is the principal advantage presented by photo-assisted electrochemical sensors when compared with the electrochemical sensors. Additionally the photo-assisted method lead to an increase of the analytical signal due to the multi-step oxidation process. The first is the chemical oxidization of dopamine from hydroxyl radicals generated on the semiconductor surface. The other process is the electrochemical oxidization of dopamine over electrode surface. These responses can be measured by the emission intensity in a specific wavelength or by the photocurrent when the nanomaterial used is a semiconductor submitted to the application of a controlled potential.

The use of semiconductor materials in photoelectrochemical sensors has become relevant in recent years [18]. Good performance of photoelectrochemical sensors depends on the optical response of the semiconductor material selected. This is related to the semiconductor band gap, where the energy of light adopted needs to be equal to or higher than the band gap to promote the translocation of electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) in the semiconductor [18].

A promising photocatalytic material under visible light irradiation that can be found in the literature is bismuth vanadate (BiVO4). BiVO4 is an n-type semiconductor. It has a band gap energy at 2.4 eV and presents the best photocatalytic performance when it is in a monoclinic crystalline structure [19]. Although the photocatalytic activity of BiVO4 is considered good when compared to TiO2 and WO3, it is worth mentioning that this activity alone does not reach its highest efficiency since recombination occurs between the photo-induced electrons in the conduction band and photo-induced holes in the valence band [20]. However, this issue can be solved by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

GQDs can increase the photocatalytic activity of BiVO4 through the removal of photo-induced electrons from the conduction band using quantum confinement [21]. Therefore, due to their advantageous properties as sensors, GQDs have been exploited by researchers to detect the most diverse biological molecules, including dopamine (DA) [22]. Based on the properties of GQDs and BiVO4 described herein, in this work, a photoelectrochemical sensor formed by a mixture of these two materials was designed in order to monitor the degree of sensitivity of the neurotransmitter DA in samples and in real time, as well as to measure the selectivity in the presence of ascorbic and uric acid.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), chronoamperometry (CA), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were performed using a model PGSTAT204 Autolab electrochemical system (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, Netherlands), coupled to an LED driver kit Autolab (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, Netherlands) and an electrochemical cell with quartz window. An FTO glass electrode modified with BiVO4/GQDs was used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl (3.0 mol L−1 KCl) was used as the reference electrode, and a platinum foil was the counter electrode. All measurements were performed with five replicates.

Ammonium vanadate (NH4VO3), dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and citric acid were obtained from Sigma/Aldrich. Isopropanol was obtained from Anidrol/Brazil. Bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) was obtained from Chem-Impex Int’l, boric acid was obtained from Merck, and potassium chloride and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Acros Organics. The supporting electrolyte was 0.1 mol L−1 borate buffer solution (pH 9.0), which was also used in the preparation of the working standard solutions by diluting the stock solutions of DA. All reagents used were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. The aqueous solutions were prepared with water purified in a Milli-Q System, Direct 8 (Millipore, USA), with resistivity ≥ 18 MΩ cm.

A mass of 1.940 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was accurately weighted, transferred to a falcon tube containing 20.0 mL of ethylene glycol, and sonicated for 40 min A mass of 0.470 g of NH4VO3 was accurately weighted and transferred to a falcon tube containing 20.0 mL of water, and heated in a water bath until complete dissolution. The latter solution was then dropped slowly and with constant stirring into the bismuth solution, causing the formation of a yellowish coloured bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) suspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal deposition of BiVO4 onto an FTO plate
ce:section.ce:para: For the deposition of bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) onto an FTO electrode, the temperature of the hot plate was set to 100 °C. Then, with the hot plate at a constant temperature, FTO was sustained until the temperature equilibrated. Thus, 30 μL of BiVO4 suspension was dropped onto 1.0 cm2 of the FTO plate. After a few minutes, a film of plastic formed. This procedure was repeated to completely cover the FTO surface. The FTO/BiVO4 system was annealed in an oven using a heating rate of 10 °C/min until reaching a temperature of 500 °C. This annealing step was maintained for 1 h. At the end of the process, the electrode was removed from the oven and allowed to cool to room temperature. Residual vanadium oxide present on the surface of the electrode after the annealing step was removed with a solution 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH using a dip bath procedure, followed by electrode washing with purified water.

An accurate mass of 1.000 g of citric acid was weighed into a beaker flask, and this reactant was heated to 200 °C for 30 min A change in the citric acid from a white solid to an orange liquid was observed. An aliquot of 50.0 mL of a 0.25 mol L−1 NaOH solution was then prepared and carefully dropped into the flask. The mixture was kept under magnetic stirring. Thus, the orange reactant was neutralized, resulting in the formation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), according to Dong et al. [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of the FTO/BiVO4 electrode with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: An aliquot containing 50 μL of GQDs was dropped onto the FTO/BiVO4 film and allowed to dry for 1 h under an infrared drying lamp, causing the formation of a transparent and homogeneous film.

The BiVO4 and BiVO4/GQDs morphologies were characterized using FEG-SEM (Field Emission Gun Scanning Electron Microscopy) microscopy. Fig. 1 A shows the typical wormlike and homogeneous film of monoclinic BiVO4 deposited on the FTO glass in which the particles of the BiVO4 film are about 180 nm, as seen in the inset of Fig. 1A. The modification of the BiVO4 film with GQDs was evaluated using HR-TEM (high-resolution transmission electron microscopy) microscopy. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the GQDs were deposited as small clusters over the BiVO4 support, and the GQD single particles are about 8 nm. Some crystalline planes of these nanoparticles can also be seen in the inset of Fig. 2 B. Observing these results it can be concluded that the GQDS were immobilized on the surface of BiVO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical behaviour of the BiVO4/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behaviour of the FTO/BiVO4 and FTO/BiVO4/GQDs surfaces were evaluated using linear sweep voltammetry in a potential range of 0.0 V to +1.0 V with a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 using an on/off step of irradiation with a hot white light LED (λ > 410 nm), as presented in Fig. 2.

In the LSV voltammograms displayed in Fig. 2, it can be observed that both electrodes, FTO/BiVO4 (curve black) and FTO/BiVO4/GQDs (curve red), exhibited a photocurrent response in the potential range studied. As expected, the BiVO4 presented a photoresponse because of the interaction of the monoclinic crystalline phase and the visible light harvesting [24,25]. However, the presence of GQDs increased the photocurrent response compared with the BiVO4 in the absence of graphene dots. This occurs because the graphene quantum dots photosensitize the BiVO4, decreasing the energy band, leading to an increase in the photocurrent response. Based on this result, it can be suggested that the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs system will be useful in the determination and quantification of dopamine.

To evaluate the electrochemical response of FTO/BiVO4/GQDs in the presence of DA, LSV experiments were carried out in borate buffer solution at pH 9.0 in the presence of 0.4 mmol L−1 dopamine standard solution over a potential range of 0.0 V to +0.9 V with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, as shown in Fig. 3 A.

It is clear to observe in Fig. 3A, it can be observed that the peak current, attributed to dopamine oxidation, increased in the presence of the light source for both electrodes compared with the same measurement in the dark. This increase in the current could be attributed to material characteristics (BiVO4 and BiVO4/GQDs). In this case, both materials presented a mechanism based on semiconductor-mediated photoelectrochemical processes. However, the presence of the GQDs on the electrode (FTO/BiVO4/GQDs, red square) increased the peak current by almost 25%, which is attributed to dopamine electrochemical oxidation, when compared with the electrode in the absence of GQDs (FTO/BiVO4, black square). The improvement in the electrochemical process caused by GQDs could be attributed to the following three major factors: First, the increase in the electrode electroactive area leads to a direct increase in the peak current. The second factor is the electrocatalytic characteristic of the GQDs [26], which displayed a shift towards more negative values in the peak potential when compared with the FTO/BiVO4 electrode. The third factor is that the GQDs can photosensitize the BiVO4. As described by Wang et al. [27], GQDs are n-type semiconductors that have narrow band gaps. Additionally, GQDs have the ability to excite electrons from the HOMO orbital to the LUMO orbital, accelerating electron transfer to the conduction band of BiVO4. In this sense, more photons could be absorbed, generating photo-induced electrons, leading to an increase in the current process.

The electrocatalytic effect of the GQDs and the increase in the electroactive area were evaluated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The spectra in Fig. 3B were, in presence of light irradiation, recorded at OCP (open circuit potential) and modelled using a modified Randles equivalent circuit (inset in Fig. 3B), consisting of the cell resistance (R s) in series with a parallel combination of a constant phase element (CPE), considered as a non-ideal capacitance of capacity (C), and a charge-transfer resistance (R ct) with a Warburg impedance (Z w).

After fitting the parameters presented in Fig. 3B, the R s values were very similar for the two different electrodes, i.e. the value found for both electrodes was close to 0.06 kΩ. The capacitances of the FTO/BiVO4 and FTO/BiVO4/GQDs electrodes were determined to be 25.4 and 66.0 μF sα−1, respectively. The increase in the capacitance value for the BiVO4/GQDs system indicates an increase in the conductive area of the surface. The electrocatalytic effect of the GQDs was verified using the R ct obtained from the equivalent circuit fitting. The FTO/BiVO4 electrode exhibited a charge transfer resistance of 2.3 kΩ and for the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs electrodes the R ct was estimated to be 0.8 kΩ. These results agree with the LSV experiments in which the electrode prepared with GQDs presented an increase in the peak current and a shift in the potential values, demonstrating the electrocatalytic effect of the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dopamine photoelectrochemical response over FTO/BiVO4/GQDs electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The photoelectrochemical response of the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs electrode was evaluated in the absence and presence of DA under light irradiation of a hot white light LED (λ > 410 nm). Fig. 4 shows displaced chronoamperometric curves that were carried out in a typical on/off system using a fixed potential of +0.4 V in the presence of 0.4 mmol L−1 DA.

The photocurrent obtained using the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs electrode presented a higher current value signal than those obtained using other studied electrodes. The current increased almost 25% compared with the electrode in absence of GQDS. This behaviour is related to the low energy needed for the electronic transitions in BiVO4 under electrode irradiation and the increase in charge transfer promoted by GQDs.
Based on the chronoamperometric response presented in Fig. 4, a schematic representation of the photoelectrochemical detection of DA is presented in Scheme 1 .

There are two simultaneous process occurring: the promotion of photo-excited electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), while the holes (h+) are generated in the VB; and the DA oxidization at controlled potential (E = +0.4 V), where the oxidized neurotransmitter acts as a collector of the holes. The electrons generated in the oxidization process combines with the h+, decreasing the intrinsic recombination between the h+ and photo-excited electrons promoted to CB. It results in the increase of the photocurrent in the semiconductor proportional to the amount of DA oxidized. This type of system was described by Gao et al. [28], but the innovation of this work is the use of the BiVO4 and the GQDs to increase the efficiency of electron transfer in photocurrent generation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Before using the FTO/BiVO4/GQDs sensor for DA analytical determination, the conditions for the method were optimized. The potential applied and the pH of the working solution were optimized for the best performance of the sensor (Figs. 1S and 2S, respectively, in ESI). The best response occurred at a potential of +0.4 V and pH of 9.0. Thus, the analytical performance for DA detection was demonstrated by an analytical curve based on the photocurrent difference (ΔI) between a blank solution and solutions with different concentrations of analyte (Fig. 5 A). The calibration curve presented a linear response (ΔI (mA) = 2.1 × 10−5 (mA) + 0.35 (mA μmol L−1) [DA] μmol L−1) (Fig. 5B) in the DA concentration range of 3.6 × 10−8 to 2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 (R2 = 0.997). Based on the IUPAC recommendation [29], the limit of detection was calculated using 3σ/S, reaching a LOD (limit of detection) value of 8.2 × 10−9 mol L−1. The limit of quantification was calculated using 10σ/S, achieving a LOQ of 2.7 × 10−8 mol L−1, where σ corresponds to the standard deviation of 10 measurements of photocurrent performed in blank solution and S is the slope of the analytical curve.

Table 1 summarizes a comparison of the analytical characteristics of different electrochemical sensors described previously for dopamine determination and the results obtained in this work. According to Table 1, comparing sensors and photo-assisted sensors developed previously, the BiVO4/GQDs composite electrode for dopamine determination shows a good limit of detection and a wide linear range. The good performance of the present sensor developed can be attributed to the effect of light irradiation allied to the intrinsic properties of the composite material developed.

The selectivity of the photosensor proposed was tested in the presence of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA), which are potential interfering substances. The signal of 4.0 μmol L−1 DA was evaluated with the responses of distinct samples with the same concentration of DA and concentrations values of AA or AU 10 and 100 times more than the neurotransmitter (Fig. 3S). The photocurrent response for the DA quantification decreased 1.5% and 2.0%, respectively, in the presence of AA under the previous conditions. For experiments in the presence of AU, the photocurrent of DA quantification decreased 3.0% and 4.0%, respectively. Based on these results, it was concluded that there was no interference of AA or AU in the DA quantification.

The presence of DA in urine is an indicator of the occurrence of diseases originating from neural cells. Therefore, its detection by the photoelectrochemical method was considered relevant in the application of the developed photosensor. Experiments for the detection of DA in the urine sample were done by the recovery method through amperometry (on/off), presenting satisfactory results at concentrations in the order of 10−7 mol L−1 (Table 2 ). For this, known amounts of DA were added to 5.0ᵡ10−3 L of 0.1 mol L―1 borate buffer solution (pH 9.0) spiked with 5.0ᵡ10−3 L of the urine. The obtained value without addition of DA standard solution corresponds to inherent amount of dopamine of the urine sample. For this reason, recovery percentages of spiked samples reached values above 100%. The application of the photoelectrochemical sensor is suitable for the UFCA screening test, considering that normal DA concentration values found in urine samples from healthy patients were around 0.16 × 10−7 mol L−1 [30].

An alternative analytical procedure was proposed in this work, in which the system for photocurrent generation through the interaction between FTO/BiVO4/GQDs and DA presented satisfactory results for sensing this neurotransmitter. The presence of GQDs was fundamental for the decrease in the charge transfer resistance, and the increase in the photocurrent signal due to the increase in the electrode electroative area and the photosensitization of the BiVO4.

The low detection limit (LOD = 8.2 × 10−9 mol L−1) allows for the application of the analytical method to detect free DA in urine from healthy humans as well as in humans with high concentrations of DA in urine, which serves as a diagnosis for several diseases. Samples did not show significant interference with the matrix.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed in part by the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brasil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001. The authors thank the grants #2017/10118-0, and grant #2016/12926-3, São Paulo Research Foundation (FAPESP), and grant #405546/2018-1 from CNPq.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.059.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Progress of Photoelectrochemical Analysis and Sensors

ACS Nano

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Thiago Martimiano do Prado received his B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from São Paulo State University, Brazil, in 2009, and his M.S. degree in 2012. He received his PhD degree in Analytical and Inorganic Chemistry from the University of São Paulo, São Paulo, SP (Brazil) in 2017. He is currently developing his postdoctoral at the Department of Chemistry of the Federal University of São Carlos. He has experience in analytical chemistry, with emphasis on electroanalysis, acting on the following topics: spectroelectrochemistry, electroanalysis, electrochemical sensor and biosensor, modified electrode, nanomaterials and hybrid materials.

Amanda Carrico: received his B.Sc. degree in Federal University of São Carlos, Brazil, in 2016 and held an internship position at Canadian Grain Commission – Canada, in 2014. She is currently developing her master’s degree researcher project at the Department of Chemistry of the Federal University of São Carlos.

Fernando Henrique Cincotto received his B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from São Paulo State University, Brazil, in 2008, and his M.S. degree in 2012. He received his PhD degree in Analytical and Inorganic Chemistry from the University of São Paulo, São Paulo, SP (Brazil) in 2016 and held an internship position from 2014 to 2015 at the Complutense University of Madrid—Spain, under the mentorship of Prof. José Manuel Pingarrón. He is currently developing his postdoctoral at the Chemistry Department of the Federal University of São Carlos. He has experience in analytical chemistry, with emphasis on electroanalysis, acting on the following topics: electroanalysis, electrochemical sensor and biosensor, modified electrode, nanomaterials and hybrid materials.

Orlando Fatibello-Filho is a full Professor in Analytical Chemistry at the Federal University of São Carlos, Department of Chemistry and, head of the LABBES (http://www.labbes.ufscar.br). He received his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from the University of São Paulo, São Paulo, SP (Brazil) in 1985 and held a postdoctoral position from 1987 to 1989 at the University of New Orleans (USA), Department of Chemistry. He was a visiting professor at University of Coimbra in the Christopher and Ana Maria Brett Group. His research interests include the development of analytical procedures employing chemical and electrochemical sensors, biosensors, solid-phase reactors, and flow-injection systems and their applications in the determination of analytes in pharmaceutical formulations as well as environmental and food samples. He has published over 280 papers with a h-index of 38 (Web of Science, August 2017) and has written 6 books and contributed 08 book chapters.

Fernando Cruz de Moraes received his B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Federal University of São Carlos, in 2002. He received his M.S. degree in 2006 from Chemistry Institute of São Carlos – University of São Paulo and Ph.D.in 2010 in Physical Chemistry from Federal University of São Carlos, in Brazil. He has been contracted in 2016 as assistant professor at Chemistry Department of the Federal University of São Carlos. His research areas of interest include electroanalysis, biosensors, environmental and biomedical analysis, synthesis and characterization of nanostructured materials based on graphene, carbon nanotubes and metallic nanoparticles. He has more than 35 peerly reviewed papers and more than 60 participations in conferences. He is a member of the International Society of Electrochemistry (ISE), and the Sociedad Iberoamericana de Electroquímica (SIBAE).
